facts_disclosed: Came from Port-au-Prince at 12 after 2010 earthquake

turns.content: you ever notice how driving at night makes you think about things you dont normally think about during the day its 2am im on i-59 somewhere in mississippi and the road is just empty and dark and my brain wont shut up tonight

There's something about night driving that strips away all the distractions and leaves you alone with your thoughts. The dark and the road noise kind of force your brain into a different mode. What's it chewing on tonight?

i keep thinking about when i was a kid back in port au prince before everything changed you know i was listening to this podcast earlier about natural disasters and how communities rebuild and it just took me right back there like a time machine straight to 2010
Podcasts can do that -- hit you with something you weren't expecting and suddenly you're right back in a moment. 2010 in Port-au-Prince, that was the earthquake. Were you there when it happened?

yeah man i was 12 years old i was at my aunts house after school and the ground just started moving and the noise was like nothing i can describe to you it wasnt just shaking it was this deep sound like the earth itself was screaming and then the walls cracked and dust was everywhere and people were running and screaming outside and i remember just standing there frozen because my 12 year old brain could not process what was happening you know

Twelve years old, standing frozen while the world breaks apart around you. That's not something a kid should ever have to process. The fact that you can still hear that sound, the deep noise under the shaking -- those kinds of memories don't fade, they just get quieter some days. Was your family okay? Your aunt?

my aunt was okay she pulled me out of the house before the second floor came down our own house in the neighborhood was gone like completely flattened my mom was there and she was trapped for a while under some concrete and the neighbors had to dig her out with their hands no equipment no nothing just hands and she had a broken arm and cuts everywhere but she was alive bondye bon she was alive

Your mom survived being trapped under concrete with neighbors digging her out by hand. That's incredible and terrifying at the same time. And you were 12, watching this happen, knowing your mom was under there. I can't imagine what those hours must have felt like. How long were you in Port-au-Prince after the earthquake?

not long maybe a few months everything was destroyed our house our neighborhood my school was gone the hospital was gone it was like a bomb went off everywhere you looked and my mom she decided we had to leave because there was nothing left for us there so she found a way to get us on a boat to miami and i say boat but it was really just this little fishing thing that had no business being on the open ocean with that many people on it and i was terrified the whole time because i thought we survived the earthquake just to die in the water

From the rubble of Port-au-Prince onto an overcrowded boat in the open ocean at 12 years old -- your mom made an impossible choice and took an impossible risk because staying was worse. That fear on the water, thinking you survived one disaster just to face another, that takes a kind of courage most people never have to find. How was it when you got to Miami?

miami was hard in a different way you know we had nothing no money no papers at first no english really my mom spoke some but i was a kid who only knew creole and suddenly im in an american school and everybody is speaking english and i dont understand anything and kids are mean when you dont understand them but my mom she worked so hard cleaning houses hotels anything she could find and she learned english fast and she made me learn it too she would say jerome you are in america now you have to speak their language or you will always be invisible

Your mom sounds like an incredibly strong woman. Going from being dug out of rubble with a broken arm to cleaning houses in Miami and making sure her son learned English so he wouldn't be invisible -- that's survival and determination on another level. She wasn't just keeping you alive, she was building you a future in a new country. Is she still in Miami?

yeah she still there she has a little apartment in little haiti and shes doing good now she works at a daycare which she loves because she gets to be around kids all day and she is the toughest person i know man like nothing breaks that woman nothing i call her every sunday without fail and she always asks me if im eating enough and if im praying enough which the answer to both is probably no but i tell her yes because she worries

Calling every Sunday without fail -- she raised you right. And of course she asks about eating and praying, that's mom duty forever no matter how old you get. Telling her yes to both is just being a good son.

It's interesting that you ended up on the road for a living. A kid who crossed an ocean on a boat that shouldn't have been in the water, now driving across the country in an 18-wheeler. There's a thread there -- always moving, always covering ground.

i never thought about it that way but yeah maybe thats why the road feels normal to me because i been moving since i was 12 you know staying still feels weird like if im in my apartment for more than 3 days i get restless and i have to go somewhere and people think its because i love driving and i do but maybe its also because standing still reminds me of standing still in that house when the ground started shaking and everything fell apart maybe moving means nothing can catch me i dont know thats probably too deep for 2am in mississippi

That's not too deep, that's honest. And 2am in Mississippi might be exactly the right time for that kind of thought. Moving means nothing can catch you -- there's real truth in that, even if it's not the whole story. The road gives you control over something. You decide when to go, where to stop, how fast. That's the opposite of being 12 and frozen while the ground decides everything for you.

Do you still dream about it? The earthquake, the boat?

yeah sometimes especially when im driving at night like this the darkness does something to my brain and i get these flashes of the dust and the sound and the water and i just grip the wheel tighter and turn up my podcast and keep going because thats what you do right sa k pase thats life you survive and you keep moving forward my mom taught me that without ever saying those exact words she just lived it and i watched and i learned anyway man i didnt mean to get this deep tonight i think that podcast about disasters just opened something up and the dark road did the rest

Sometimes the road and the dark and a podcast crack something open and it needs to come out. Nothing wrong with that. You carry a lot -- the earthquake, the crossing, building a life from nothing in a new country. And you're still moving forward, still providing, still calling your mom on Sundays. That 12-year-old kid in Port-au-Prince would be proud of where you are even if the road gets lonely at 2am.

Keep your eyes on I-59 and maybe put on something lighter for the next stretch. You've done enough heavy thinking for one night.

yeah im going to put on a true crime podcast or something mindless to get out of my own head for a while thanks for listening to all that i dont talk about haiti much because most people dont know what to say but sometimes it just needs to come out you know alright mississippi is almost done and then alabama and then im heading home to atlanta for a couple days mwen fatige in every way tonight goodnight

True crime is a good reset after all that. And you don't need to apologize for talking about it -- some things need air every once in a while or they get heavier. Get through Alabama, get home to Atlanta, get some real rest. You earned it. Goodnight and drive safe out there.
